---
title: "010 — SHEV: the structurally isolated technology"
date: 2026-06-12
---

## Question

Strong hybrids (SHEVs) are the most fuel-efficient non-plug-in technology on
sale in India — yet they idle near 2% share while EVs and CNG climb S-curves.
Consumer preference, or **structural isolation**: no FAME eligibility, ~43%
effective GST (vs 5% for EVs), and — as we quantify below — literally zero
nodes in the policy-incentive graph. (Background: *SHEV* card in the
[guide](guide.qmd).)

## Setup

```{python}
import duckdb
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
from complexity_lab.config import settings
from complexity_lab.analysis.econometrics import did

con = duckdb.connect(str(settings.db_path), read_only=True)
HAS_WS = "wholesale" in {r[0] for r in con.execute("SHOW TABLES").fetchall()}
```

## 1 — The incentive graph has no SHEV node

```{python}
pol = con.execute("SELECT label, detail FROM ref_policy_events").df()
text = (pol["label"].fillna("") + " " + pol["detail"].fillna("")).str.lower()
counts = pd.Series({
    "EV": int(text.str.contains("ev|electric").sum()),
    "CNG": int(text.str.contains("cng").sum()),
    "Strong Hybrid": int(text.str.contains("hybrid").sum()),
})
px.bar(counts, title="Policy events touching each technology (lab events database)",
       labels={"index": "technology", "value": "events"}).update_layout(showlegend=False)
```

GST adds the price wedge: EVs 5%, small cars 28%+1–3% cess, **strong hybrids
28% + 15% cess ≈ 43%** (compiled from GST Council notifications). The most
efficient ICE-adjacent technology carries the heaviest consumption tax.

## 2 — Trajectories (and a measurement trap)

```{python}
shares = con.execute(
    "SELECT date, ev_share, cng_share, hybrid_share FROM panel_state_month "
    "WHERE state_code = 'ALL' AND year >= 2021 ORDER BY date").df()
fig = px.line(shares, x="date", y=["ev_share", "cng_share", "hybrid_share"],
              title="National fuel shares — note hybrid 'appears' only in 2024")
fig.add_vline(x="2024-01-01", line_dash="dot",
              annotation_text="Vahan fuel-classification break")
fig
```

::: {.callout-warning}
Vahan records `Strong Hybrid` only from 2024 — the zero before that is a
**classification artifact**, not zero sales. Wholesale has **no fuel cut**. The
view below uses externally assigned model metadata to show hybrid-associated
nameplates from 2017; it cannot split mixed-powertrain model quantities.
:::

```{python}
if HAS_WS:
    wsf = con.execute(
        "SELECT date, wholesale FROM ws_fuel_month WHERE fuel = 'Hybrid' "
        "AND date >= '2022-04-01' ORDER BY date").df()
    px.area(wsf, x="date", y="wholesale",
            title="Hybrid-associated nameplate dispatches (not a fuel cut) — "
                  "Hycross/Invicto/Camry ramp from late 2022").show()
```

## 3 — The one place a SHEV incentive existed: UP's waiver

Uttar Pradesh waived registration tax on strong hybrids in **July 2024** — the
single SHEV-specific incentive node in the period. A natural experiment:

```{python}
mp = con.execute(
    "SELECT state_code, date, hybrid_share FROM panel_state_month "
    "WHERE year >= 2023 ORDER BY date").df()
CONTROLS = ["RJ", "MP", "BR", "WB", "GJ", "KA", "TN", "PB"]
res = did(mp, treated="UP", controls=CONTROLS, event_date="2024-07-01",
          value_col="hybrid_share", pre_months=6, post_months=8)
print(f"ATT = {100*res['att']:+.2f}pp  (UP change {100*res['treated_change']:+.2f}pp "
      f"vs controls {100*res['control_change']:+.2f}pp); "
      f"placebo rank p = {res['placebo_rank_p']:.2f} "
      f"(floor with {len(CONTROLS)} controls = {1/(len(CONTROLS)+1):.2f})")
```

```{python}
plot = mp[mp["state_code"].isin(["UP", *CONTROLS])].copy()
plot["group"] = plot["state_code"].where(plot["state_code"] == "UP", "controls")
g = plot.groupby(["group", "date"])["hybrid_share"].mean().reset_index()
fig = px.line(g, x="date", y="hybrid_share", color="group",
              title="UP vs control states — hybrid share around the Jul-2024 waiver")
fig.add_vline(x="2024-07-01", line_dash="dash", annotation_text="UP waiver")
fig
```

```{python}
pl = pd.Series(res["placebos"], name="pseudo_att").mul(100).sort_values()
px.bar(pl, title="Placebo ATTs (each control treated as if it had the waiver) vs UP",
       labels={"value": "pseudo-ATT (pp)", "index": "state"}
       ).add_hline(y=100*res["att"], line_dash="dash", line_color="#A4243B",
                   annotation_text="UP actual")
```

## Finding

The isolation thesis survives every test we can throw at it with public data:
**zero policy nodes** touch SHEVs (vs ten for EVs), the GST wedge prices the
technology like a luxury rather than an efficiency play, and the single
incentive that did exist — UP's registration waiver — produced a **~+2.3pp
hybrid-share lift** that exceeds every placebo state's pseudo-effect. The
binding constraint looks fiscal, not preferential. *Paper roadmap*: add the
Bass counterfactual (re-simulate adoption with the EV-equivalent 5% GST price
position) and the formal policy-network diagram; venue per blueprint §17.2.
